mnemonics.title: D.E.C. - Decode Everything Completely
mnemonics.content: Decoder: n inputs → 2^n outputs. Only ONE output is HIGH at a time. Think: 'Decode' = 'Decipher' one unique output from binary input.

mnemonics.title: E.N.C. - Encode Number Code
mnemonics.content: Encoder: 2^n inputs → n outputs. Converts active HIGH input to binary code. Priority Encoder handles multiple HIGH inputs (highest gets priority).

mnemonics.title: M.U.X. - Many U to X (cross)
mnemonics.content: Multiplexer: 2^n data inputs + n select lines → 1 output. Select lines choose which data input passes through. Think: 'Many into One'.

mnemonics.title: DEMUX - Demultiplexer
mnemonics.content: Demultiplexer: 1 input + n select lines → 2^n outputs. Routes input to one specific output based on select lines. Opposite of MUX.

mnemonics.title: Select Seven Save Time
mnemonics.content: Remember: For 2^n inputs/outputs, you need n select lines. More select lines = more inputs/outputs but saves separate wiring.

acronyms.usage: Use when you need to select one data input from multiple sources and send it to a single output line.
Use when you need to take one input and route it to one of many possible output lines.
Use when converting 2-bit binary to 4 possible unique outputs (one active at a time).

acronyms.expansion: 4 data inputs to 1 output using 2 select lines
acronyms.usage: Use when selecting 1 of 4 data sources; 2 select lines (00,01,10,11) choose which input passes.
Use when converting 8 possible inputs (one HIGH at a time) to 3-bit binary code.

acronyms.expansion: Handles multiple HIGH inputs by prioritizing highest-order input
acronyms.usage: Use in interrupt handling or when multiple inputs can be active simultaneously.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hotel with floor numbers (binary input). Each floor has ONE unique room light (output) that turns on. 2 inputs = 4 floors = only ONE floor's light ON at a time.
Picture 8 emergency buttons (inputs) in a building. When one is pressed, a 3-digit floor code (binary output) displays. Priority encoder: if multiple pressed, highest floor wins.
Think of multiple train tracks (data inputs) meeting at one track. The signal operator (select lines) chooses which train passes onto the main line - ONE train at a time.
One train (input) arrives at a junction. The yard master (select lines) directs it to ONE specific platform (output) from multiple platforms.
Always visualize the power of 2: 1 select line → 2 inputs/outputs; 2 select lines → 4 inputs/outputs; 3 select lines → 8 inputs/outputs. Draw the pyramid!

analogies.analogy: Think of a postal sorting system. A 2-digit postal code (input) opens only ONE specific sorting bin (output) - each bin has a unique number. The code '00' opens bin 0, '01' opens bin 1, etc.
Like a 911 emergency system. When multiple people call (8 phone lines = inputs), the system converts the highest priority call into a 3-digit location code (binary output) for dispatch.
Like a DJ at a party with multiple music players. The DJ (select lines) chooses which player's music plays through the main speakers (single output) at any moment.
Like a water sprinkler with multiple garden zones. One main water pipe (input) is directed by valves (select lines) to water ONLY ONE zone (output) at a time.
Active HIGH is like a light switch that turns ON when you PUSH (1). Active LOW is like a doorbell that rings when you PUSH but circuit is 'waiting' - conceptually inverting the trigger.
